1: Standard revelation formula (vayehi devar YHWH elai): 'The word of YHWH came to me.' This formula recurs ~50 times in Ezekiel, marking each oracle as direct prophetic reception. Unlike prophets who say 'the oracle of YHWH,' Ezekiel consistently uses the dabar (word) reception formula, emphasizing that what follows is verbal divine communication rather than visionary experience.
2: mah-yitron etz ha-gafen mikol-etz: 'What advantage does the wood of the vine have over any [other] wood?' Yitron (advantage, profit) is a Qoheleth term (Eccl 1:3) pressed into prophetic service here — the question is commercial/utilitarian. Etz ha-gafen (vine wood) was widely understood in Israel as Israel's symbol: Ps 80 calls Israel YHWH's vine, Isa 5 develops the vineyard parable, Hos 10:1 uses vine imagery. Ezekiel shocks by taking the symbol and asking: what is vine wood actually good for as a material? The answer is: nothing at all.
3: hayitkach mimenu etz lamelachah — im-yikchu mimenu yated litlot alav keli: 'Is wood taken from it for any work? Can one make a peg from it to hang a vessel on?' Melachah (work, craftsmanship, skilled labor) is the same word used in Exod 31:3 for the skilled craftsmanship of Bezalel. Vine wood cannot serve even the simplest carpentry purpose. The rhetorical double question (will it be used for work? can you even make a peg from it?) intensifies the uselessness. The vine's value lies entirely in its fruit; stripped of its fruit-bearing function, it is less than any other wood.
4: hineh la-esh nitana le-akal: 'Behold it is given to the fire for fuel.' Akal (eating, consuming) describes the fire's appetite — the vine wood is consumed completely. The verse adds that fire consumes both ends and chars the middle — even partially burned vine wood has no residual use. The image is complete consumption with no remainder, no recycling, no value retrieved from the waste.
5: ap ki hayo shlemah lo yeaseh lemalacha: 'Even when it was whole, it was not used for work — how much less, after the fire has consumed it and it is charred, can it be used for any work?' The rhetorical af ki (how much more/less) is a qal va-homer argument: if the vine was useless in its best condition (whole, unburned), the charred remnant is even more useless. This sets up the application in vv. 6-8: if Jerusalem was spiritually useless even before judgment, she will be completely useless after it.
6: lachem ken natati et-yoshevei Yerushalayim: 'Therefore thus I have given the inhabitants of Jerusalem.' The application of the parable is stark: the vine wood given to fire = Jerusalem given to judgment. The perfect tense (natati, I have given) treats the judgment as already certain — prophetic perfect. The transition from parable to application with lachem (therefore) is typical of Ezekiel's mashal-interpretation pattern (cf. ch. 17 and 24).
7: venathati et-panai bam: 'I will set my face against them.' The panim (face) of YHWH formula appears in blessings (Num 6:25, YHWH's face shining) and in judgments. Setting the face against someone is the opposite of the Aaronic blessing — it signals sustained divine opposition. The phrase implies the withdrawal of protective favor. The paradox that they escape one fire only to be consumed by another reflects the relentlessness of the judgment: there is no refuge from the fire that is YHWH's own judgment.
8: vahaarets tihyeh leshamamah: 'The land shall become a desolation.' Shamamah (desolation, waste) is Ezekiel's signature judgment word (cf. 6:14; 12:20; 33:28). The root shamam conveys both the physical waste of the land and the horror/astonishment it produces in those who see it. The closing formula (ne'um adonai YHWH, declares the Lord YHWH) seals the oracle — Ezekiel uses this double divine name throughout to emphasize YHWH's sovereign authority behind the judgment word.
